title: Singularities in Mixed Characteristic
speaker: Linquan Ma
abstract:
Simonyi 101 and Remote Access
Singularities are local properties of algebraic varieties. For example, the solution set of a polynomial in several variables such as y2=x3
 has a singularity at the origin (0,0)
. In algebra, one often studies singularities through the quotient ring, such as C[x,y]/y2−x3
 for this example. In this talk, we will give an introduction to recent progress in the study of singularities in mixed characteristic, roughly speaking, these correspond to quotients of Z[x1,...,xn]
 by polynomials with integer coefficients. A central theme in this study is the use of so-called big Cohen-Macaulay algebras, these are often "large" (non-Noetherian) rings that nevertheless enjoys remarkably nice homological properties  (and whose existence is related to recent developments in p-adic Hodge theory). We also discuss some applications of the mixed characteristic singularity theory to questions in birational geometry.

proof_stages.label: Local singularities and mixed-characteristic setting
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes commutative rings, explains singularities through local coordinate rings, and motivates studying quotient rings over p-adic or integral bases rather than only rings containing a field.

proof_stages.label: Rational singularities in characteristic zero
proof_stages.summary: Rational singularities are recalled via resolutions and vanishing of higher direct images, with examples, nonexamples, and the practical difficulty of checking the condition directly.

proof_stages.label: F-rationality and reduction modulo p
proof_stages.summary: The positive-characteristic bridge is introduced through reduction modulo p, local cohomology, Frobenius action, F-rational singularities, and practical Fedder-type criteria for checking them.

proof_stages.label: Single-prime criterion for rational singularities
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the Schwede–Ma theorem that one F-rational reduction modulo a single prime suffices for rational singularities in characteristic zero, yielding a semi-algorithmic test using positive-characteristic computations.

proof_stages.label: Big Cohen-Macaulay algebra technology
proof_stages.summary: Big Cohen-Macaulay algebras are defined by local-cohomology vanishing, related to the direct summand theorem, and supplied in mixed characteristic by deep existence and absolute-integral-closure results.

proof_stages.label: BCM-rational singularities as a character-free bridge
proof_stages.summary: BCM-rational singularities are defined using injections of top local cohomology into all big Cohen-Macaulay algebras, and are positioned as equivalent to F-rationality in characteristic p and as implying rational singularities in characteristic zero.

proof_stages.label: Proof of the one-prime theorem by inversion of adjunction
proof_stages.summary: The proof passes to an integral model, uses F-rationality of the mod-p fiber as BCM-rationality, lifts it by an inversion-of-adjunction statement for a nonzerodivisor, and then specializes to rational singularities on the characteristic-zero generic fiber.

proof_stages.label: Applications to mixed-characteristic birational geometry
proof_stages.summary: The BCM framework and related vanishing results are described as contributing to progress on the minimal model program for threefolds in mixed characteristic, with limitations coming from resolution and vanishing failures in higher dimension or small characteristic.

examples.name: Plane curve singularities: parabola, node, and cusp
examples.note: The pictures and coordinate rings of a smooth parabola, a node, and a cusp make the basic notion of singularity concrete, and the later question notes that for plane curves rational singularities are only the smooth ones.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the plane curves visually near the origin and writes their concrete coordinate rings/equations, later relating plane-curve rational singularities to smoothness

examples.name: Affine cone over a projective variety
examples.note: The affine cone shows why singularities arise even from smooth projective varieties, gives a concrete nonexample criterion for rational singularities, and later illustrates the local-cohomology calculation.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes/describes the local cohomology of an affine cone as a direct sum of sheaf cohomologies and states a concrete obstruction to rational singularities

passes.reason: Affine cones are standard constructions, and cones with nonzero higher structure-sheaf cohomology are used as non-examples of rational singularities.
